what: Split data into training and testing
title: Time Series Cross Validation with M4 Monthly Data
text: prepare data set time series cross-validation (TSCV), can use function make_split(). function splits data several slices training testing (.e. partitioning time slices) time series cross-validation. can choose stretch slide. first expanding window approach, latter fixed window approach. Furthermore, define (initial) window size training testing via n_init n_ahead, well step size increments via n_skip. options splitting data available via type (see function reference details). vignette, use fixed window approach 120-month training window 18-month testing window. value n_skip = 17 creates non-overlapping 18-month test windows.
code: # Setup for time series cross validation type = "first" value = 120       # size for training window (= 10 years of monthly observations) n_ahead = 18      # size for testing window (= forecast horizon, 18 months ahead) n_skip = 17       # skip 17 observations to obtain non-overlapping test windows n_lag = 0         # no lag mode = "slide"    # fixed window approach exceed = FALSE    # only pseudo out-of-sample forecast  split_frame <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = type,   value = value,   n_ahead = n_ahead,   n_skip = n_skip,   n_lag = n_lag,   mode = mode,   exceed = exceed )  split_frame #> # A tibble: 13 × 4 #>    series split train       test       #>    <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #>  1 M10960     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  2 M10960     2 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  3 M10960     3 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  4 M10960     4 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  5 M10960     5 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  6 M10960     6 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  7 M10960     7 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  8 M10960     8 <int [120]> <int [18]> #>  9 M10960     9 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 10 M10960    10 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 11 M10960    11 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 12 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 13 M23100     2 <int [120]> <int [18]>

title: Time Series Cross Validation with M4 Monthly Data
text: training test splits prepared within split_frame ready forecasting. function slice_train() slices data main_frame according splits within split_frame. using forecasting methods packages fable fabletools, convert data set main_frame tibble tsibble. monthly M4 data, use simple benchmark automatic forecasting methods: NAIVE: naive random walk model SNAIVE: seasonal naive model yearly seasonality STL-NAIVE: STL-decomposition model naive forecast. series decomposed via STL seasonally adjusted series predicted via naive approach. Afterwards, seasonal component added forecasts ETS: exponential smoothing state space model ARIMA: automatic ARIMA model SMEDIAN: seasonal median model yearly seasonality function SMEDIAN() extension fable package.

title: Time Series Cross Validation with M4 Monthly Data
text: evaluate forecast accuracy, function make_accuracy() used. can define whether evaluate accuracy horizon split. Several accuracy metrics available: : mean error MAE: mean absolute error MSE: mean squared error RMSE: root mean squared error MAPE: mean absolute percentage error sMAPE: scaled mean absolute percentage error MPE: mean percentage error rMAE: relative mean absolute error (relative user-defined benchmark method)

title: Time Series Cross Validation with M4 Monthly Data
code: # Estimate accuracy metrics by forecast horizon accuracy_horizon <- make_accuracy(   future_frame = future_frame,   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   dimension = "horizon" )  accuracy_horizon #> # A tibble: 1,512 × 6 #>    series model dimension     n metric value #>    <chr>  <chr> <chr>     <int> <chr>  <dbl> #>  1 M10960 ARIMA horizon       1 MAE     10.4 #>  2 M10960 ARIMA horizon       2 MAE     10.8 #>  3 M10960 ARIMA horizon       3 MAE     12.8 #>  4 M10960 ARIMA horizon       4 MAE     20.2 #>  5 M10960 ARIMA horizon       5 MAE     23.9 #>  6 M10960 ARIMA horizon       6 MAE     27.3 #>  7 M10960 ARIMA horizon       7 MAE     30.3 #>  8 M10960 ARIMA horizon       8 MAE     28.9 #>  9 M10960 ARIMA horizon       9 MAE     30.5 #> 10 M10960 ARIMA horizon      10 MAE     32.6 #> # ℹ 1,502 more rows  # Visualize results accuracy_horizon |>   filter(metric == "MAE") |>   plot_line(     x = n,     y = value,     facet_var = series,     facet_nrow = 1,     color = model,     title = "Evaluation of forecast accuracy by forecast horizon",     subtitle = "Mean absolute error (MAE)",     xlab = "Forecast horizon (n-step-ahead)",     caption = "Data: M4 Competition, own calculation"     )

what: Split data into training and testing
title: Expanding window approach
text: prepare data time series cross-validation, use function make_split(). function partitions data several training testing slices. argument mode controls type rolling-origin resampling: mode = "stretch" creates expanding window. mode = "slide" creates fixed window. expanding window approach, training sample grows time. first split starts initial training window fixed length. later splits, additional observations added training sample, forecast horizon remains unchanged. vignette, use: value = 120: first training window contains 120 monthly observations, corresponding 10 years data n_ahead = 18: test set contains 18 observations, corresponding 18-month-ahead forecast horizon n_skip = 17: split, rolling origin advanced 18 months, creates non-overlapping test windows mode = "stretch": training window expands time resulting object split_frame contains one row per time series split. columns train test list-columns containing row positions used training test samples. output shows first split uses 120 observations training 18 observations testing. second split, training window expanded 138 observations, test window remains fixed 18 observations. third split series M14395 uses 156 observations training. number splits can differ across time series selected M4 series necessarily number observations. example , M14395 allows three valid 18-month test windows, M23100 allows two.
code: # Setup for time series cross validation type = "first" value = 120       # initial training window (= 10 years of monthly observations) n_ahead = 18      # testing window (= forecast horizon, 18 months ahead) n_skip = 17       # skip 17 observations to obtain non-overlapping test windows n_lag = 0         # no lag mode = "stretch"  # expanding window approach exceed = FALSE    # only pseudo out-of-sample forecast  split_frame <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = type,   value = value,   n_ahead = n_ahead,   n_skip = n_skip,   n_lag = n_lag,   mode = mode,   exceed = exceed )  split_frame #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   series split train       test       #>   <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #> 1 M14395     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 2 M14395     2 <int [138]> <int [18]> #> 3 M14395     3 <int [156]> <int [18]> #> 4 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 5 M23100     2 <int [138]> <int [18]>

title: Expanding window approach
text: training test samples defined split_frame. now use slice_train() slice_test() extract corresponding observations main_frame. Since forecasting models estimated functions fable fabletools, training data converted tibble tsibble. monthly M4 data, estimate three forecasting models: SNAIVE: seasonal naive model yearly seasonality ETS: exponential smoothing state space model ARIMA: automatic ARIMA model models estimated separately time series split. object model_frame mable, , model table. row corresponds one combination series split, model stored separate column. model specification may change across splits models re-estimated training sample. especially visible automatic ETS() ARIMA() models. example, selected ARIMA specification M14395 differs split 1, split 2 split 3. expected, since split uses different training sample. seasonal period [12] ARIMA output indicates yearly seasonality monthly data.

title: Expanding window approach
text: visualize rolling forecasts, combine observed values forecasts one data set. training test observations labelled ACTUAL, forecasts keep model names. observed values assigned horizon = 0, whereas forecast values horizons 1 18. object plot_frame useful plotting actual observations forecasts together. Since data contains several rolling-origin splits, facet plots split.   plots show forecasts evolve across different rolling-origin splits. facet, training window ends different point time. models estimated using available training data split produce forecasts following 18 months. use expanding window approach, later splits based larger training samples. can lead changes estimated model structure resulting forecasts.

title: Expanding window approach
text: vignette demonstrated use tscv time series cross-validation expanding window approach. workflow consists five main steps: Prepare data define column context. Create rolling-origin splits using make_split(). Extract training test samples using slice_train() slice_test(). Estimate forecasting models create forecasts. Visualize evaluate rolling forecasts. expanding window approach useful training sample grow time, often case real-world forecasting applications available historical information used model estimation.

what: Split data into training and testing
title: Time Series Cross Validation
text: prepare data set time series cross-validation (TSCV), can use function make_split(). function splits data several slices training testing (.e. partitioning time slices) time series cross-validation. can choose stretch slide. first expanding window approach, latter fixed window approach. Furthermore, define (initial) window size training testing via n_init n_ahead, well step size increments via n_skip. options splitting data available via type (see function reference details).
code: # Setup for time series cross validation type = "first" value = 2400      # size for training window n_ahead = 24      # size for testing window (= forecast horizon) n_skip = 23       # skip 23 observations n_lag = 0         # no lag mode = "slide"    # fixed window approach exceed = FALSE    # only pseudo out-of-sample forecast  split_frame <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = type,   value = value,   n_ahead = n_ahead,   n_skip = n_skip,   n_lag = n_lag,   mode = mode,   exceed = exceed )  # For illustration, only the first 50 splits are used split_frame <- split_frame %>%   filter(split %in% c(1:50))  split_frame #> # A tibble: 200 × 4 #>    bidding_zone split train         test       #>    <chr>        <int> <list>        <list>     #>  1 DE               1 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  2 DE               2 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  3 DE               3 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  4 DE               4 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  5 DE               5 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  6 DE               6 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  7 DE               7 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  8 DE               8 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  9 DE               9 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #> 10 DE              10 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #> # ℹ 190 more rows

title: Time Series Cross Validation
text: training test splits prepared within split_frame ready forecasting. function slice_train() slices data main_frame according splits within split_frame. using forecasting methods packages fable fabletools, convert data set main_frame tibble tsibble. Due sample size computation time, simple benchmark methods used: SNAIVE: Seasonal naive model weekly seasonality (package fable) STL-NAIVE: STL-decomposition model naive forecast. series decomposed via STL seasonal adjusted series predicted via naive approach. Afterwards, seasonal component added forecasts (packages fable feasts) SNAIVE2: Variation seasonal naive approach. Mondays, Saturdays Sundays treated weekly lag. Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays Fridays treated daily lag. SMEDIAN: Seasonal median model functions SMEDIAN() SNAIVE2() extensions fable package

title: Time Series Cross Validation
text: evaluate forecast accuracy, function make_accuracy() used. can define whether evaluate accuracy horizon split. Several accuracy metrics available: : mean error MAE: mean absolute error MSE: mean squared error RMSE: root mean squared error MAPE: mean absolute percentage error sMAPE: scaled mean absolute percentage error MPE: mean percentage error rMAE: relative mean absolute error (relative user-defined benchmark method)

what: Split data into training and testing
title: Fixed window approach
text: prepare data time series cross-validation, use function make_split(). function partitions data several training testing slices. argument mode controls type rolling-origin resampling: mode = "stretch" creates expanding window. mode = "slide" creates fixed window. fixed window approach, size training sample remains constant time. split, training window moves forward fixed number observations. Older observations drop training sample newer observations added. useful recent observations expected relevant older observations, data-generating process may change time. vignette, use: value = 2400: training window contains 2,400 hourly observations n_ahead = 24: test set contains 24 observations, corresponding 24-hour-ahead forecast horizon n_skip = 23: split, rolling origin advanced 24 hours, creates non-overlapping test windows mode = "slide": training window slides forward keeping fixed size argument exceed = FALSE ensures pseudo --sample forecasts generated. words, test windows created corresponding observations available data. resulting object split_frame contains one row per time series split. columns train test list-columns containing row positions used training test samples. mode = "slide" used, training set length 2,400 observations. first split uses first 2,400 observations training following 24 observations testing. next split moves training window forward 24 hours. training sample still contains 2,400 observations, oldest 24 observations dropped next 24 observations added. first 50 splits used vignette keep example computationally manageable.
code: # Setup for time series cross validation type = "first" value = 2400      # size for training window n_ahead = 24      # size for testing window (= forecast horizon) n_skip = 23       # skip 23 observations n_lag = 0         # no lag mode = "slide"    # fixed window approach exceed = FALSE    # only pseudo out-of-sample forecast  split_frame <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = type,   value = value,   n_ahead = n_ahead,   n_skip = n_skip,   n_lag = n_lag,   mode = mode,   exceed = exceed )  # For illustration, only the first 50 splits are used split_frame <- split_frame %>%   filter(split %in% c(1:50))  split_frame #> # A tibble: 200 × 4 #>    bidding_zone split train         test       #>    <chr>        <int> <list>        <list>     #>  1 DE               1 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  2 DE               2 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  3 DE               3 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  4 DE               4 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  5 DE               5 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  6 DE               6 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  7 DE               7 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  8 DE               8 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #>  9 DE               9 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #> 10 DE              10 <int [2,400]> <int [24]> #> # ℹ 190 more rows

title: Fixed window approach
text: training test splits prepared within split_frame, now ready forecasting. function slice_train() extracts training observations main_frame according row positions stored split_frame. Since forecasting models estimated functions fable fabletools, training data converted tibble tsibble. key consists bidding zone split number, model estimated separately bidding zone rolling-origin split. Due sample size computation time, vignette uses simple benchmark methods: SNAIVE: seasonal naive model weekly seasonality STL-NAIVE: STL decomposition model combined naive forecast seasonally adjusted series SNAIVE2: variation seasonal naive approach; Mondays, Saturdays Sundays treated weekly lag, Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays Fridays treated daily lag SMEDIAN: seasonal median model functions SNAIVE2() SMEDIAN() extensions fable package provided tscv. object model_frame model table, mable. row corresponds one combination bidding zone split. model stored separate column. forecasts created forecast(h = n_ahead), produces 24 hourly forecasts model, bidding zone split. function make_future() converts resulting fable object regular tibble can used evaluation functions tscv.

title: Fixed window approach
text: evaluate forecast accuracy, use function make_accuracy(). function compares forecasts future_frame observed values main_frame. Accuracy can evaluated along different dimensions: dimension = "horizon" summarizes forecast errors forecast horizon. shows forecast accuracy changes 1-step-ahead 24-step-ahead forecasts. dimension = "split" summarizes forecast errors rolling-origin split. shows whether forecast origins difficult others. Several accuracy metrics available: : mean error MAE: mean absolute error MSE: mean squared error RMSE: root mean squared error MAPE: mean absolute percentage error sMAPE: symmetric mean absolute percentage error MPE: mean percentage error rMAE: relative mean absolute error, relative user-defined benchmark method

title: Fixed window approach
text: Accuracy forecast horizon useful understanding forecast performance changes forecast horizon increases. example, short-term electricity price forecasts may accurate next hours later hours following day.  resulting plot compares mean absolute error across forecast horizons bidding zone model. Increasing values indicate forecasts become less accurate forecast horizon. Differences models show benchmark performs best different horizons.
code: # Estimate accuracy metrics by forecast horizon accuracy_horizon <- make_accuracy(   future_frame = future_frame,   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   dimension = "horizon" )  accuracy_horizon #> # A tibble: 2,688 × 6 #>    bidding_zone model   dimension     n metric value #>    <chr>        <chr>   <chr>     <int> <chr>  <dbl> #>  1 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       1 MAE     4.84 #>  2 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       2 MAE     5.05 #>  3 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       3 MAE     4.85 #>  4 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       4 MAE     4.50 #>  5 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       5 MAE     6.73 #>  6 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       6 MAE     6.66 #>  7 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       7 MAE     6.40 #>  8 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       8 MAE     6.73 #>  9 DE           SMEDIAN horizon       9 MAE     7.38 #> 10 DE           SMEDIAN horizon      10 MAE     9.06 #> # ℹ 2,678 more rows  # Visualize results accuracy_horizon %>%   filter(metric == "MAE") %>%   plot_line(     x = n,     y = value,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     facet_nrow = 1,     color = model,     title = "Evaluation of forecast accuracy by forecast horizon",     subtitle = "Mean absolute error (MAE)",     xlab = "Forecast horizon (n-step-ahead)",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency, own calculation"     )

title: Fixed window approach
text: Accuracy split useful identifying forecast origins models perform relatively well poorly. can reveal periods unusual price behavior, strong volatility, negative prices sudden price spikes.  resulting plot compares mean absolute error across rolling-origin splits. Large error values particular split indicate corresponding 24-hour test window difficult forecast. Comparing models across splits helps assess whether forecast performance stable time.

title: Fixed window approach
text: vignette demonstrated use tscv time series cross-validation fixed window approach. workflow consists four main steps: Prepare data define column context. Create fixed rolling-origin splits using make_split(). Estimate forecasting models separately time series split. Evaluate forecast accuracy horizon split. fixed window approach useful training sample represent recent period constant length. can particularly helpful time series electricity prices, seasonal patterns, volatility market conditions may change time.

title: Visualization of time series data
text: data set elec_price tibble day-ahead electricity spot prices [EUR/MWh] ENTSO-E Transparency Platform. data set contains hourly time series data 2019-01-01 2020-12-31 eight European bidding zones. vignette, use four bidding zones: DE: Germany, including Luxembourg FR: France NO1: Norway 1, Oslo SE1: Sweden 1, Lulea visualization functions tscv work data long format. Therefore, define context object identifies relevant columns: series_id: column identifying individual time series value_id: column containing numeric measurement variable index_id: column containing time index
code: series_id = "bidding_zone" value_id = "value" index_id = "time"  context <- list(   series_id = series_id,   value_id = value_id,   index_id = index_id )  # Prepare data set main_frame <- elec_price %>%   filter(bidding_zone %in% c("DE", "FR", "NO1", "SE1"))  main_frame #> # A tibble: 70,176 × 5 #>    time                item            unit      bidding_zone  value #>    <dttm>              <chr>           <chr>     <chr>         <dbl> #>  1 2019-01-01 00:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            10.1  #>  2 2019-01-01 01:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -4.08 #>  3 2019-01-01 02:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -9.91 #>  4 2019-01-01 03:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -7.41 #>  5 2019-01-01 04:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE           -12.6  #>  6 2019-01-01 05:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE           -17.2  #>  7 2019-01-01 06:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE           -15.1  #>  8 2019-01-01 07:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -4.93 #>  9 2019-01-01 08:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -6.33 #> 10 2019-01-01 09:00:00 Day-ahead Price [EUR/MWh] DE            -4.93 #> # ℹ 70,166 more rows

title: Visualization of time series data
text: Line charts common visualization time series data. show observed values change time useful detecting trends, seasonal patterns, level shifts, outliers periods high volatility. function plot_line() creates line charts data long format. first example creates faceted plot, one panel bidding zone.   faceted version useful individual time series different levels volatility. combined version useful comparing bidding zones directly single panel.
code: # Example 1 -------------------------------------------------------------------  main_frame %>%   plot_line(     x = time,     y = value,     color = bidding_zone,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     subtitle = "2019-01-01 to 2020-12-31",     xlab = "Time",     ylab = "[EUR/MWh]",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     ) # Example 2 -------------------------------------------------------------------  main_frame %>%   plot_line(     x = time,     y = value,     color = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     subtitle = "2019-01-01 to 2020-12-31",     xlab = "Time",     ylab = "[EUR/MWh]",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     )

title: Visualization of time series data
text: Bar charts can used display summary values category lag. example, use plot_bar() visualize sample partial autocorrelation function. partial autocorrelation function measures relationship time series lagged values controlling intermediate lags. often used exploratory tool identify relevant lag structures time series models. First, estimate sample partial autocorrelation function using estimate_pacf(). argument lag_max = 30 computes partial autocorrelation lags 1 30.  resulting correlogram shows estimated partial autocorrelation lag bidding zone. variable sign indicates whether absolute value estimated partial autocorrelation exceeds approximate confidence bound used estimate_pacf().
code: # Estimate sample partial autocorrelation function corr_pacf <- estimate_pacf(   .data = main_frame,   context = context,   lag_max = 30 )  corr_pacf #> # A tibble: 120 × 6 #>    bidding_zone type    lag    value  bound sign  #>    <chr>        <chr> <int>    <dbl>  <dbl> <lgl> #>  1 DE           PACF      1  0.948   0.0124 TRUE  #>  2 DE           PACF      2 -0.437   0.0124 TRUE  #>  3 DE           PACF      3  0.00539 0.0124 FALSE #>  4 DE           PACF      4  0.0735  0.0124 TRUE  #>  5 DE           PACF      5  0.0735  0.0124 TRUE  #>  6 DE           PACF      6  0.0898  0.0124 TRUE  #>  7 DE           PACF      7  0.119   0.0124 TRUE  #>  8 DE           PACF      8  0.0786  0.0124 TRUE  #>  9 DE           PACF      9  0.0820  0.0124 TRUE  #> 10 DE           PACF     10  0.0469  0.0124 TRUE  #> # ℹ 110 more rows  # Visualize PACF as correlogram corr_pacf %>%   plot_bar(     x = lag,     y = value,     color = sign,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     position = "dodge",     title = "Sample autocorrelation function",     xlab = "Lag",     ylab = "Correlation",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"   ) #> Warning in geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position, aes(x = {: #> Ignoring unknown parameters: `size`

title: Visualization of time series data
text: Distribution plots useful understanding marginal distribution observed values. electricity prices, particularly relevant prices may show skewness, heavy tails, negative values extreme spikes. following examples use histograms, density plots QQ-plots explore distribution hourly electricity prices across bidding zones.

title: Visualization of time series data
text: Histograms show frequency distribution observed values. useful identifying range, central tendency, skewness outliers time series. first example overlays distributions four bidding zones one plot.   faceted histogram separates bidding zones individual panels. makes easier inspect distribution time series separately, especially distributions overlap combined plot.
code: # Example 1 -------------------------------------------------------------------  main_frame %>%   plot_histogram(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "[EUR/MWh]",     ylab = "Frequency",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     ) #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`. # Example 2 -------------------------------------------------------------------  main_frame %>%   plot_histogram(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     facet_nrow = 1,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "[EUR/MWh]",     ylab = "Frequency",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     ) #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

title: Visualization of time series data
text: Density plots provide smoothed version empirical distribution. Compared histograms, often easier use comparing several distributions one figure.   combined density plot highlights differences bidding zones location spread prices. faceted version provides clearer view individual distribution.
code: # Example 1 ------------------------------------------------------------------- main_frame %>%   plot_density(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "[EUR/MWh]",     ylab = "Density",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     ) # Example 2 ------------------------------------------------------------------- main_frame %>%   plot_density(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     facet_nrow = 1,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "[EUR/MWh]",     ylab = "Density",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     )

title: Visualization of time series data
text: QQ-plots compare empirical distribution observed values theoretical distribution, usually normal distribution. useful checking whether data approximately normally distributed. electricity prices, deviations normality common prices can skewed may contain extreme values.   observations approximately normally distributed, points QQ-plot lie close straight line. Strong deviations pattern indicate skewness, heavy tails outliers.
code: # Example 1 ------------------------------------------------------------------- main_frame %>%   plot_qq(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "Theoretical Quantile",     ylab = "Sample Quantile",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     ) # Example 2 ------------------------------------------------------------------- main_frame %>%   plot_qq(     x = value,     color = bidding_zone,     facet_var = bidding_zone,     title = "Day-ahead Electricity Spot Price",     xlab = "Theoretical Quantile",     ylab = "Sample Quantile",     caption = "Data: ENTSO-E Transparency"     )

Line charts help inspect temporal structure data, distribution plots correlograms help identify features may relevant modelling forecasting.

text: package tscv provides helper functions time series analysis, forecasting, time series cross-validation. designed work tidy forecasting ecosystem, especially tsibble, fable, fabletools, feasts. package contains tools : creating rolling-origin resampling schemes time series cross-validation slicing training test samples time-indexed data converting forecasts common format evaluation calculating forecast accuracy measures visualizing time series data, forecast errors, distributional properties fitting additional benchmark forecasting models compatible fable working example time series data sets main focus package simplify repeated forecasting experiments across several time series, models, forecast horizons, rolling-origin splits.

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: can install development version GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("ahaeusser/tscv")

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: typical workflow tscv consists following steps: Prepare data long format. Define context object identifying series, value, index columns. Create rolling-origin splits using make_split(). Slice training test data using slice_train() slice_test(). Estimate forecasting models using fable additional models provided tscv. Convert forecasts make_future(). Evaluate forecast accuracy make_accuracy(). Visualize data, forecasts, accuracy measures.

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: central idea time series cross-validation evaluate forecasts repeatedly time. Instead relying single train-test split, tscv creates several rolling-origin splits. split contains training window model estimation test window forecast evaluation. Two common rolling-origin schemes fixed window expanding window cross-validation.

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: fixed window setup, training window test window move forward time. length training sample stays constant. useful recent observations expected informative older observations, example data-generating process changes time. plot , row represents one split square represents one time point. Blue squares used training, dark squares used testing, light squares used split. Fixed window cross-validation: training test windows move forward time. example, following code creates fixed window splits one monthly time series M4 forecasting competition. first training window contains 120 monthly observations test window contains next 18 observations. Since origin advances one step, n_skip = 17 moves next split forward 18 observations total. resulting object contains one row per split. train test columns list-columns row positions used model estimation forecast evaluation.
code: context <- list(   series_id = "series",   value_id = "value",   index_id = "index" )  main_frame <- M4_monthly_data |>   filter(series == "M23100")  fixed_split <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = "first",   value = 120,   n_ahead = 18,   n_skip = 17,   n_lag = 0,   mode = "slide",   exceed = FALSE )  fixed_split #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   series split train       test       #>   <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #> 1 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 2 M23100     2 <int [120]> <int [18]>

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: expanding window setup, start training sample stays fixed end moves forward time. training sample therefore grows split. useful historical observations considered informative goal mimic forecasting process data becomes available time. Expanding window cross-validation: training window grows test window moves forward. setup can switched expanding window cross-validation using mode = "stretch". approaches use forecast horizon rolling-origin step size. difference training sample updated. splits created, can passed remaining tscv workflow: slice training test samples, estimate forecasting models, convert forecasts, evaluate forecast accuracy.
code: expanding_split <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = "first",   value = 120,   n_ahead = 18,   n_skip = 17,   n_lag = 0,   mode = "stretch",   exceed = FALSE )  expanding_split #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   series split train       test       #>   <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #> 1 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 2 M23100     2 <int [138]> <int [18]>

title: Functions and Utilities for Tidy Time Series Forecasting and Time Series Cross-Validation
text: creating split plan, training test samples can extracted used tidy forecasting ecosystem. example fits seasonal naive model split, converts forecasts standardized format, calculates forecast accuracy horizon.
code: train_frame <- slice_train(   main_frame = main_frame,   split_frame = expanding_split,   context = context ) |>   as_tsibble(     index = index,     key = c(series, split)   )  model_frame <- train_frame |>   model(     "SNAIVE" = SNAIVE(value ~ lag("year"))   )  fable_frame <- model_frame |>   forecast(h = 18)  future_frame <- make_future(   fable = fable_frame,   context = context )  accuracy_frame <- make_accuracy(   future_frame = future_frame,   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   dimension = "horizon" )  accuracy_frame #> # A tibble: 126 × 6 #>    series model  dimension     n metric value #>    <chr>  <chr>  <chr>     <int> <chr>  <dbl> #>  1 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       1 MAE      130 #>  2 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       2 MAE      160 #>  3 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       3 MAE      115 #>  4 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       4 MAE      145 #>  5 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       5 MAE      125 #>  6 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       6 MAE      125 #>  7 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       7 MAE      150 #>  8 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       8 MAE      110 #>  9 M23100 SNAIVE horizon       9 MAE      100 #> 10 M23100 SNAIVE horizon      10 MAE      125 #> # ℹ 116 more rows

text: check_data() data preparation helper time series workflows. performs three tasks: checks data tsibble; checks time index regular ordered key index; optionally turns implicit missing values explicit missing values   using fill_gaps(). input data key variables, treated wide data converted long format. resulting output contains original index column, variable column containing former column names, value column containing corresponding observations. title: Estimate kurtosis — estimate_kurtosis
text: function computes moment-based kurtosis $$   \frac{n \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^4}        {\left(\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2\right)^2} $$ Missing values removed default. returns usual kurtosis, excess kurtosis. normal distribution kurtosis close 3.

title: Estimate kurtosis — estimate_kurtosis
code: x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, NA)  estimate_kurtosis(x) #> [1] 1.7 estimate_kurtosis(x, na_rm = TRUE) #> [1] 1.7  set.seed(123) y <- rnorm(100) estimate_kurtosis(y) #> [1] 2.838947

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
text: Estimate skewness numeric distribution.

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
code: estimate_skewness(x, na_rm = TRUE)

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
text: x Numeric vector. na_rm Logical value. TRUE, missing values removed estimation.

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
text: numeric value giving estimated skewness.

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
text: function computes moment-based skewness $$   \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^3}        {\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2\right)^{3/2}} $$ Missing values removed default. Positive values indicate distribution longer heavier right tail; negative values indicate distribution longer heavier left tail.

title: Estimate skewness — estimate_skewness
code: x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 10, NA)  estimate_skewness(x) #> [1] 1.13842 estimate_skewness(x, na_rm = TRUE) #> [1] 1.13842  set.seed(123) y <- rexp(100) estimate_skewness(y) #> [1] 2.790016

text: future_frame tibble containing point forecasts. must contain columns specified context, well model, split, horizon, point. main_frame tibble containing observed values. must contain series identifier, time index, value column specified context. context named list identifiers series_id, value_id, index_id. dimension Character value. Determines dimension accuracy summarized. Common choices "split" "horizon". benchmark Optional character value giving model name used benchmark relative mean absolute error rMAE.

title: Estimate point forecast accuracy — make_accuracy
text: tibble containing forecast accuracy metrics. output contains series identifier, model name, selected dimension, dimension value n, metric name, metric value.

title: Estimate point forecast accuracy — make_accuracy
text: make_accuracy() compares point forecasts future_frame observed values main_frame. two data sets joined using series identifier time index defined context. Accuracy can summarized along different cross-validation dimensions: dimension = "split" summarizes accuracy separately   test split. dimension = "horizon" summarizes accuracy separately   forecast horizon. following point forecast accuracy metrics returned: : mean error. MAE: mean absolute error. MSE: mean squared error. RMSE: root mean squared error. MAPE: mean absolute percentage error. sMAPE: symmetric mean absolute percentage error. MPE: mean percentage error. benchmark supplied, function also computes relative mean absolute error rMAE. rMAE calculated model's MAE divided MAE benchmark model series selected dimension.

title: Create train-test splits for time series cross-validation — make_split
text: main_frame tibble containing time series data. context named list identifiers series_id, value_id, index_id. type Character value. type initial split. Possible values "first", "last", "prob". value Numeric value specifying initial split. n_ahead Integer. forecast horizon, .e. number observations test window. n_skip Integer. number observations skip split origins. default 0. n_lag Integer. number lagged observations include test window. useful lagged predictors required constructing test features. default 0. mode Character value. Either "slide" fixed-window approach "stretch" expanding-window approach. exceed Logical value. TRUE, --sample splits exceeding original sample size created.

title: Create train-test splits for time series cross-validation — make_split
text: tibble containing split plan. output one row per time series split, list-columns train test containing integer row positions.

title: Create train-test splits for time series cross-validation — make_split
text: make_split() creates rolling-origin train-test splits time series cross-validation. output used functions slice_train() slice_test() extract corresponding training testing samples main_frame. function supports two training-window modes: mode = "slide" creates fixed-window approach. training   window constant length moves forward time. mode = "stretch" creates expanding-window approach.   training window starts first observation grows time. initial training window controlled type value: type = "first" uses first value observations   initial training window. type = "last" keeps last value observations   testing derives initial training window remaining sample. type = "prob" uses floor(value * n_total) observations   initial training window. argument n_skip controls far rolling origin moves consecutive splits. non-overlapping test windows, use n_skip = n_ahead - 1.

title: Create train-test splits for time series cross-validation — make_split
code: library(dplyr)  context <- list(   series_id = "series",   value_id = "value",   index_id = "index" )  main_frame <- M4_monthly_data |>   filter(series == "M23100")  # Fixed-window split plan fixed_split <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = "first",   value = 120,   n_ahead = 18,   n_skip = 17,   n_lag = 0,   mode = "slide",   exceed = FALSE )  fixed_split #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   series split train       test       #>   <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #> 1 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 2 M23100     2 <int [120]> <int [18]>  # Expanding-window split plan expanding_split <- make_split(   main_frame = main_frame,   context = context,   type = "first",   value = 120,   n_ahead = 18,   n_skip = 17,   n_lag = 0,   mode = "stretch",   exceed = FALSE )  expanding_split #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   series split train       test       #>   <chr>  <int> <list>      <list>     #> 1 M23100     1 <int [120]> <int [18]> #> 2 M23100     2 <int [138]> <int [18]>

title: Create indices for train and test splits — split_index
text: n_total Integer. total number observations time series. n_init Integer. number observations initial training window. n_ahead Integer. forecast horizon, .e. number observations test window. n_skip Integer. number observations skip split origins. default 0. n_lag Integer. number lagged observations include test window. default 0. mode Character value. Either "slide" fixed-window approach "stretch" expanding-window approach. exceed Logical value. TRUE, test indices may exceed original sample size.

title: Create indices for train and test splits — split_index
text: split_index() creates integer index vectors rolling-origin resampling. function can create either fixed-window expanding-window splits: mode = "slide" creates fixed training window moves   forward time. mode = "stretch" creates expanding training window   always starts first observation. first training window contains n_init observations. test window contains n_ahead observations. argument n_skip controls many observations skipped consecutive split origins. example, n_ahead = 18 n_skip = 17, consecutive test windows non-overlapping. n_lag > 0, test indices include lagged observations forecast horizon. useful lagged predictors needed constructing features testing. exceed = TRUE, additional --sample test indices allowed exceed original sample size.
